content.text: A blameless postmortem is a structured review of an incident that focuses on

, not individual performance. The goal is to extract learning that prevents the next incident — not to assign responsibility for the last one. This topic defines the complete postmortem template for GenAI production incidents, the timeline for producing it, and the facilitation techniques that keep the conversation blameless and productive.

attrs.alt: Blameless postmortem structure and timeline showing seven document sections on the left (summary, timeline, impact, root cause, what went well, action items, learning points) and the delivery timeline on the right (T+0 to T+30 days)

content.text: The blameless postmortem principle — originating in SRE practice at Google — holds that engineers in complex systems
make the best decisions they can with the information available at the time
. When an incident happens, the interesting question is not 'who made a mistake?' but 'what conditions made the mistake easy to make and hard to catch?'
No naming individuals in the root cause section ('the on-call engineer' not 'Alice')
Framing every failure as a system or process gap, not a personal failure
Separating the postmortem meeting from performance review discussions
Explicitly welcoming 'I thought X was someone else's job' — that is a process gap, not a confession
Publishing findings openly across the engineering organisation, not treating them as embarrassing

mean consequence-free. Repeated negligence or deliberate violations of procedures are handled through the normal performance management process — not the postmortem. The postmortem is specifically for learning, not discipline.
No exceptions; written even if duration was short
May be shorter format if < 30 min and fully understood
High learning value; often skipped in practice
Legal review of the published version required
Repeated SEV3+ of same type within 90 days
Raw timestamped log in incident channel
Draft doc with timeline + preliminary root cause
Internal wiki page, linked from Jira incident
All items closed, rescheduled, or explicitly deferred
All-hands slide or monthly postmortem digest
Use this template for every SEV1 and SEV2. Sections are mandatory; depth is proportional to incident complexity.

# Postmortem: INC-2024-03-15-042

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Incident ID** | INC-2024-03-15-042 |
| **Title** | Inference API P99 Latency > 30s |
| **Severity** | SEV1 |
| **Duration** | 39 minutes (14:18 – 14:57 UTC) |
| **Detection** | Automated alert (MTTD: 2 minutes) |
| **IC** | Alice |
| **Responders** | Dave (ML), Eve (Infra), Carol (Comms) |
| **Customer impact** | ~40% of inference requests in us-east-1a |
| **SLO error budget consumed** | 18% of monthly error budget |
| **Postmortem author** | Alice |
| **Review meeting** | 2024-03-17 10:00 UTC |
| **Status** | Draft / In Review / Published |

The timeline is the most important section. It must be factual, timestamped, and complete. The scribe log from the incident channel is the primary source — supplement it with monitoring data and responder recollection.

## Timeline

| Time (UTC) | Event | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 14:16 | Inference pod us-east-1a-pod-3 OOMKilled (first occurrence) | k8s events |
| 14:17 | Pod restarts begin on us-east-1a-pod-4 and pod-5 | k8s events |
| 14:18 | Alert `InferenceAPIHighLatency` fires: P99 > 5s | PagerDuty |
| 14:20 | On-call engineer Alice acknowledges alert; declares SEV1 | PagerDuty |
| 14:20 | Incident channel #inc-2024-03-15-042 created | Slack |
| 14:23 | Bridge opened; Dave and Eve join | Slack |
| 14:25 | P99 reaches 34.2s peak | Grafana |
| 14:28 | Dave identifies OOM pattern on 3/5 us-east-1a pods | Bridge |
| 14:32 | Eve checks Helm values; finds memory limit 4Gi vs 8Gi actual requirement | Git + Helm |
| 14:35 | DECISION: Cordon us-east-1a and reroute to us-west-2 | Bridge |
| 14:40 | Cordon applied; traffic rerouting begins | kubectl |
| 14:45 | P99 drops below 2s | Grafana |
| 14:55 | P99 < 2s for 10 consecutive minutes; error rate < 0.1% | Grafana |
| 14:57 | SEV1 resolved; bridge closed | Slack |
| 15:10 | Memory limit corrected in Helm values; PR opened | GitHub |
| 15:45 | PR merged; config deployed to us-east-1a | GitHub + k8s |
| 16:00 | us-east-1a traffic restored | k8s |

## Impact Assessment

### User impact
- Approximately 40% of inference API requests in us-east-1a returned 503 or timed out
- Affected period: 14:18 – 14:55 UTC (37 minutes of active impact)
- Peak error rate: 42% of requests
- Peak P99 latency: 34.2 seconds

### SLO impact
- Monthly availability SLO: 99.9% (target), 99.87% (actual this month after incident)
- Error budget consumed: 18% of monthly budget in 39 minutes
- SLO violated? No — still within 99.9% monthly budget

### Customer impact
- Enterprise customers with us-east-1a primary workloads: 12 accounts affected
- Self-serve users: ~3,400 users with failed requests in the window
- SLA breaches: 2 enterprise customers (SLA threshold: 99.95%)

### Revenue impact
- Estimated failed request value: $1,400 (based on token pricing)
- SLA credits owed: $8,200 (2 enterprise contracts)
Use the 5 Whys technique to get beyond the proximate cause to the systemic failure. Each 'why' should be a

## Root Cause Analysis

### Proximate cause
Inference pods in us-east-1a were OOMKilled due to a memory limit configuration
lower than actual runtime requirements.

### 5 Whys

**Why** did the inference pods OOMKill?
→ The Helm memory limit was set to 4Gi, but pods require 8Gi under production load.

**Why** was the memory limit incorrect?
→ A Helm values update 3 days prior changed the limit without reference to the
  current memory profile data.

**Why** was the incorrect limit deployed without detection?
→ Our CI pipeline validates Helm syntax but does not validate semantic correctness
  against runtime memory profiles.

**Why** was there no runtime alert before the OOMKill?
→ We have a memory pressure alert threshold, but it was tuned for the old (4Gi) limit
  rather than the actual requirement. The alert fired at the same time as the OOMKill.

**Why** did we not catch this in staging?
→ Staging workloads run at 20% of production load and did not trigger the memory
  pressure that causes the OOMKill at production scale.

### Root cause
Absence of Helm memory limit validation against production memory profiles in CI,
combined with staging environment that does not replicate production load characteristics.

### Contributing factors
- Alert threshold tuned to configured limit rather than actual requirement
- No change freeze or extra review for configuration changes in the week before a
  scheduled major model deployment
- The Helm change was a 'minor' update and bypassed the standard infrastructure
  review process
This section is often skipped or treated as perfunctory. It is not. Identifying what worked cements those practices and highlights skills worth spreading to the rest of the team.

## What Went Well

- **Alert MTTD: 2 minutes.** The `InferenceAPIHighLatency` alert fired within 2 minutes
  of the first OOMKill. The alert threshold and on-call response were both fast.

- **Diagnosis speed.** The team correctly identified OOM as the root cause within 12
  minutes of the bridge opening, and traced it to the Helm config within 20 minutes.
  This is faster than our median diagnosis time for memory-related incidents (35 minutes).

- **Decision under uncertainty.** The IC made the cordon-and-reroute decision at T+17
  minutes (relative to declaration) before the root cause was fully confirmed. This was
  the right call — isolating the blast radius while diagnosis continued.

- **Scribe log quality.** The scribe log was complete and timestamped correctly, making
  this postmortem faster to write than average.

- **Customer comms timing.** The first status page update was posted at T+28 (within
  the T+30 window). The enterprise customer email went at T+32 (slightly late but within
  acceptable range given the active mitigation deployment).
Every action item must have an
. Vague items like 'improve monitoring' are not action items — they are aspirations. Actionable items are specific, verifiable, and owned.

## Action Items

| # | Action | Owner | Due | Jira | Type |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Add Helm memory limit validation against production memory profile to CI | @dave | 2024-03-29 | PLAT-1102 | Prevent |
| 2 | Tune memory pressure alert threshold to actual requirement, not configured limit | @eve | 2024-03-22 | OBS-447 | Detect |
| 3 | Implement automated memory limit drift detection (alert if config < p95 runtime) | @eve | 2024-04-05 | OBS-448 | Detect |
| 4 | Add load-profile injection to staging to replicate production memory pressure | @dave | 2024-04-12 | PLAT-1103 | Detect |
| 5 | Automate regional traffic rerouting on OOMKill detection | @infra-team | 2024-04-19 | INFRA-892 | Mitigate |
| 6 | Require infrastructure review for Helm value changes in production | @eng-lead | 2024-03-25 | ENG-301 | Prevent |

Action types: **Prevent** (stops recurrence), **Detect** (catches it faster), **Mitigate** (reduces blast radius)

## Learning Points

These are the transferable insights from this incident that apply beyond the specific fix:

1. **Configuration validation must be semantic, not syntactic.** Helm linting catches
   format errors but not semantic mismatches between configured limits and runtime
   behaviour. Any infrastructure-as-code change should be validated against runtime
   profiles, not just schema.

2. **Alert thresholds must track the thing that matters, not the configuration.**
   Our memory pressure alert was tuned to the configured limit rather than the actual
   requirement. Alert thresholds should be derived from observed runtime behaviour
   (p95 of actual memory use), not from configuration values.

3. **Staging under-load is a systemic detection gap.** Configuration issues that
   only manifest under production-level load will not be caught in staging unless
   staging replicates that load. Consider load profile injection as standard practice.

4. **The 'minor change' label is an incident risk factor.** The Helm update was
   classified as minor and bypassed standard review. Classify changes by risk
   (memory, CPU, network changes are never 'minor' in production) not by size.

. The document is written before the meeting. The meeting is where the team validates the timeline, reaches agreement on root cause, and generates action items together. Without a meeting, the document is just one person's retrospective.
IC, all primary responders, EM, optional: skip-level manager
Skip-level presence signals importance; should listen, not judge
IC is too close to the incident to facilitate objectively
Same video call; shared document open
Everyone should be able to edit simultaneously
Recording changes what people say; decide case by case
— State blameless ground rules; confirm this is a systems review, not a performance review
— Walk the timeline chronologically; responders correct and add context; scribe updates doc in real-time
— Facilitator leads; ask 'what condition made this possible?' not 'who caused this?'
— Structured round; everyone contributes at least one item
— For each proposed item: assign an owner and a date before moving to the next
— Facilitator asks: what should every engineer know after this?
Facilitation Phrases for Keeping It Blameless
Someone says 'X should have known better'
"Let's focus on what information was available at that point and what the process gap was."
Discussion gets stuck on who did what
"We can document the sequence in the timeline. What condition made that decision the most likely one?"
Someone is visibly reluctant to share
"There are no wrong answers in timeline corrections — we need the real sequence to learn from it."
"Before we move on — who owns this specifically? 'The team' is not an owner."
"We're at the time limit. Let's capture remaining items as open questions and schedule a follow-up if needed. We cannot skip action items."
AI systems have failure modes that require additional postmortem sections beyond what a standard SRE postmortem covers:
Which model version was active? What inputs triggered degraded outputs? Was the degradation gradual or sudden?
What safety filters failed or were bypassed? What was the content? How many users received it?
Was the vector store corrupted? Did retrieval return wrong context? Was the embedding model version consistent?
Did hallucination rate spike correlate with a specific input pattern? Is there a reproducible test case?
Does our fallback to backup provider work? Was the failover automatic or manual? How long did it take?
A postmortem that is written but not shared is wasted learning. Publish to:
Internal engineering wiki — searchable; referenced in future incidents of the same type
Monthly postmortem digest — circulated to all engineers, not just the incident team
All-hands engineering meeting — high-impact incidents deserve 5-10 minutes of verbal discussion
External status page (PIR) — for enterprise customers and public transparency

Postmortems should never be marked confidential except when they contain legally sensitive information (safety incidents, security breaches). The practice of 'private postmortems' is incompatible with a learning culture — it signals that incidents are embarrassing rather than educational.
Every SEV1 and SEV2 requires a blameless postmortem; safety incidents always require one regardless of severity
Blameless means focusing on system and process conditions, not individual performance — distinct from consequence-free
The seven document sections: summary header, timeline, impact, root cause (5 Whys), what went well, action items, learning points
Timeline: draft at T+24h, meeting at T+48h, published internally at T+5 business days, action items reviewed at T+30 days
Every action item needs a named owner, a specific due date, and a Jira ticket — 'improve monitoring' is an aspiration, not an action item
The postmortem meeting is facilitated by a neutral party; the IC is too close to the incident to facilitate fairly
GenAI incidents require additional sections for model behaviour, safety evaluation, RAG analysis, and provider dependency
Postmortems must be published and shared — a postmortem no one reads produces no learning
